summary: Justin Brennan walks through a pitch deck for a 33-unit Kansas City apartment deal closing in 30 days, explaining how to structure and present investor opportunities at stages 3-4 (after deal acquisition and underwriting). The mentees (Hayden and Matt) are raising ~$730k with a 6% preferred return, 16-18% projected returns, and $25k minimum investment, demonstrating how pitch decks answer 90% of investor questions.
takeaways: Pitch deck is created at stages 3-4 (after offer accepted, in due diligence/escrow) to raise capital from your investor database
Minimum investment threshold: $25,000 in this deal structure
Preferred return: 6% baseline guaranteed return, included in total 16-18% projected return
80/20 equity split: investors receive 80% of profits, GP takes 20%
Equity multiple 1.99-2.25x means $100k investment returns $199k-$225k total
GP puts in 10% skin-in-game on smaller deals (33-50 units); reduces to ~5% on larger $30M+ deals
If raised at least 50% of equity by end of 30-day due diligence period when money goes hard, keep going; if raised nothing/very little, consider canceling to protect deposit
Soft commitments: build investor database before deals close using CRM; gather contact info from conversations (backyard barbecues, retail encounters, etc.)
Create placeholder pitch deck as marketing tool even before first deal to warm prospects and build rapport
Legal documents (PPM, contracts) prepared when entering escrow; use attorney templates from portal then connect with PPM lawyers for final review/naming
Solicit both accredited and non-accredited investors via 506(b) offering; 506(c) requires accredited-only but allows public online advertising
Start raising money simultaneously with deal sourcing via soft commitments, not after deal is found
Pitch deck template pulls 80% content from broker OM (marketing package) via copy-paste; use ChatGPT for market statistics/charts/graphs
Can backfill capital after closing if needed: close deal with partial equity raise then continue raising remaining capital post-close

skill_candidates.change: Begin building soft-commitment investor database via informal conversations (CRM tracking) before any deal is closed rather than waiting until deal is found
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin emphasizes: 'what you should be doing is getting what's called soft commitments' via CRM and website presence. Shares Trader Joe's example where casual conversation led to $50k investor. Advises: 'You're kind of collecting people. You could even start that now.'

skill_candidates.change: Use fixed-rate interest-only bridge debt (2-3 year terms) or fixed 6-7% financing instead of conservative underwriting when market conditions force aggressive bidding on competitive deals
skill_candidates.evidence: Brian mentioned striking out on deals because institutional buyers using bridge debt with interest-only terms. Justin responds: 'ask them too I'd say hey is there any kind of interest-only, you know, two or three-year type debt that's fixed rate...that could be an option' and 'the lenders in the back end of the system will...can provide that financing.'

skill_candidates.change: Specify when legal documents (PPM, contracts, investor agreements) must be prepared: at escrow entry, not before, to reduce pre-closing costs that will be reimbursed at closing
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin clarifies: 'you would start that pretty much when you go into escrow' and 'the closing cost of the deal includes the cost of your documents. There may need to be a little bit of prefunding on that, right, to get it going, but it will be reimbursed to you.'

title: Tuesday Mentorship Q&A_05-20-25 | Justin Brennan
